planting.note: Direct sow in fall into dry, sandy or calcareous, well-drained soil; lightly scarify seeds before sowing to improve germination rates and encourage spring bloom.

directions: The tallest of the Texas bluebonnets, Big Bend Bluebonnet is a native of dry, rocky and sandy desert washes in the Trans-Pecos and Big Bend region of far west Texas, producing dramatic upright spikes of deep blue lupine flowers on stems reaching 4 feet or more in March and April. Direct sow seeds in fall on bare, sandy or rocky, freely draining soil in full sun; lightly scarify or nick seed coats before planting to break dormancy and speed germination. This species thrives in dry, low-humidity climates and is not suited to the humid eastern portions of Texas, performing best in calcareous, well-drained soils of the Hill Country and Trans-Pecos. It does not transplant well and self-sows reliably in suitable desert garden and xeriscape conditions once established.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Lupinus havardii
Wildflower Center Native Plant Database, University of Texas at Austin

planting.note: Direct sow on bare, sandy soil in fall; nick or soak seeds overnight before sowing to encourage germination and winter-into-spring bloom.

directions: Arizona Lupine is a vivid native annual of sandy desert flats, bajadas, and dry washes across southern Arizona, the Mojave and Sonoran deserts of California, and adjacent Mexico, producing silky-haired palmate foliage and dense spikes of deep magenta-pink to rose-purple flowers from February through April. It is one of the most spectacular wildflowers of the Colorado Desert during superbloom years, blanketing open sandy ground with color. Direct sow in fall on bare, dry, sandy or gravelly, freely draining soil in full sun; nick or soak seeds beforehand to improve germination rates. This species does not transplant well and should be established in place, where it will self-sow reliably in subsequent seasons once it has set seed.
sources: Arizona-Sonora Desert Museum Plant Care Guide

planting.note: Direct sow on bare, dry, sandy or gravelly soil in fall; scarify or soak seeds to break seed coat dormancy before sowing into desert wildflower beds or native mixes.

directions: Bajada Lupine is one of the smallest native annual lupines in North America, forming low, dense clusters of tiny palmate leaves and producing small but charming spikes of lavender to bicolored blue-and-white flowers across sandy bajadas, desert flats, and gravelly washes of the Mojave and Sonoran deserts in winter and early spring. It is a cheerful and common participant in California desert superbloom events and mixes well with other small desert annuals such as desert bluebell and desert gold poppy. Sow seeds in fall directly onto bare, dry, sandy or gravelly, freely draining soil in full sun; scarify or soak seeds overnight before planting to speed germination. This species self-sows readily in suitable conditions and is widely available through California native seed suppliers for use in native and desert wildflower mixes.

planting.note: Plant young transplants in spring into gritty, rocky, freely draining soil in a crevice or rock garden; spores may be surface-sown on moist, sterile medium in late winter indoors.

directions: Mountain Woodsia is a compact, tufted native fern of rocky cliff ledges, talus slopes, and gravelly mountain outcrops throughout North America from Alaska to the southern Appalachians, producing small pinnate fronds 6-18 inches tall with soft glandular hairs that give the plant a slightly sticky, textured appearance. It is highly adapted to thin, rocky, freely draining soil and exposed, dry mountain conditions, making it an outstanding choice for crevice gardens, dry-stone walls, and alpine rock garden collections in cool climates. Plant young divisions or transplants in spring into gritty, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky soil in partial shade to full sun; protect from excessive heat and humidity, as this species is not suited to gardens below USDA zone 7. Water sparingly once established, as consistently moist or heavy soil will cause crown rot.
sources: Flora of North America — Woodsia scopulina
American Fern Society — Rock Ferns for the Garden

planting.note: Divide established clumps and transplant in early spring before fronds fully unfurl; plant into moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade.

directions: Glade Fern is an elegant, upright native fern of moist, shaded rocky woodland glades, limestone bluffs, and humus-rich forest floors throughout eastern North America, producing erect once-pinnate fronds with long, narrow lance-shaped pinnae that give the plant an unusually slender, airy, almost grass-like silhouette among woodland ferns. This distinctive narrow texture makes it an outstanding contrast plant alongside broad-leaved shade perennials such as hosta, ligularia, and Solomon's seal in woodland and shade garden settings. Plant in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; amend heavy clay soils with leaf mold or compost before planting to ensure good drainage and aeration. Established plants tolerate brief summer dryness but perform best with consistent moisture and benefit from a mulch of shredded leaves to retain soil moisture through the growing season.
sources: Flora of North America — Athyrium pycnocarpon
North American Rock Garden Society — Native Ferns for Shade Gardens
